macro_segments.title: Blood of Chatat on Leather Before and After Flaying: The Debate Between Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Eliezer

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Halacha: Blood of Chatat on Garment Requiring Laundering in the Azarah
Distinguishing Types of Material: Garment, Sackcloth, and Leather Before Flaying
Rabbi Yehuda's Position: Leather Requires Laundering Only After Being Flayed
Rabbi Eliezer's Position: Even Flayed Leather Does Not Require Laundering
Summary of Criteria for Laundering Blood of Chatat

macro_segments.title: Talmudic Derivation: The Gezeira Shava and the Category of Objects That Require Laundering

micro_segments.title: Baraita: Deriving the Inclusion of Leather Through Textual Bracketing and Gezeira Shava
Rabbi Yehuda's Reading: Anything Fit to Receive Tumah Requires Laundering
Rabbi Eliezer's Reading: Only What Actually Receives Tumah Requires Laundering
The Distinction Between Raui (Fit) and Mekabbel (Actually Receiving): Intent and Machshava

macro_segments.title: Examples of Machshava Determining Tumah Status: Patches, Garments, and Floor Mats

micro_segments.title: Abaye's Example: Small Cloth Patch Requiring Intent to Become Fit for Tumah
Rava's First Example: Finished Garment Held Back from Tumah by Intention to Embroider
Rava's Second Example: Leather Floor Mat Awaiting Trimming
Rabbi Shimon ben Menasya's Teaching: Intent to Trim Prevents Purity Status

macro_segments.title: The Three Criteria for Laundering Blood: Location, Capability for Tumah, and Suitability for Laundering

micro_segments.title: First Criterion: Only the Exact Spot Where Blood Splattered Requires Laundering
Second Criterion: Object Must Be Fit to Receive Tumah (Raui l'Kabbel Tumah)
Third Criterion: Object Must Be Suitable for Laundering, Not Scraping
Consensus View: Garment, Sackcloth, and Leather All Require Laundering

macro_segments.title: The Question of Laundering Leather: Distinguishing Soft and Hard Leather, and the Role of Vigorous Rubbing

micro_segments.title: Challenge to Leather Laundering: Tanna Kama and Acherim Debate Whether Water on Leather Constitutes Laundering
Rav's Practice: Cleaning Muddy Shoes on Shabbat by Applying Water
Resolution by Abaye: Differentiating Between Tanna Kama (Hard Leather) and Acherim (Soft Leather)
Rava's Refined Position: The Critical Role of Kishkush (Vigorous Rubbing) in Determining Laundering
Clarification: Wet Fabric Automatically Constitutes Laundering; Leather Requires Kishkush
Final Position: Soft Leather Only Problematic with Kishkush; Hard Leather Debated
Rav Pappa's Challenge and Rava's Retraction: Clarifying Kishkush as Prohibited, Not Rinsing
Practical Ruling: Rinsing Permitted, Vigorous Rubbing Forbidden; Uncertainty on Hard Leather

macro_segments.title: Extension to Other Halachos: Breaking Earthenware Vessels and Rinsing Metal Vessels in the Azarah

micro_segments.title: Breaking Earthenware: Application of the Holy Place Requirement to Contaminated Pottery
Rinsing Metal Vessels: Kashering Copper Vessels in the Azarah
Uniqueness of Chatat: Stringency of Blood Halacha Applies Only to Chatat, Not Other Sacrifices

macro_segments.title: Exploring the Stringency of Chatat: Attempts to Define What Makes Chatat More Weighty Than Other Sacrifices

micro_segments.title: The Stringencies Considered: Blood Entering the Heichal, Inner vs. Outer Chatat, Atonement for Karet, Four Applications
Rejection of Proposed Stringencies: Rabbi Akiva's Rule Applies Broadly; Not All Chatasim Atone for Karet
The Blood Halacha as a Particular Stringency: Why This Specific Halacha is Unique to Chatat
